answers.value:
I am requesting approval to take an excess credit load for the upcoming semester in order to complete my remaining degree requirements and stay on track to graduate at the end of this academic year.

I am a junior in the Management Information Systems (MIS) program in the Eberly College of Business and Information Technology. After meeting with my advisor, we reviewed my DegreeWorks audit and confirmed that I have a manageable but slightly overloaded combination of remaining major requirements, Business core courses, and a minor requirement in Data Analytics. Because of when certain upper-level MIS and Business core courses are offered (many of them only in specific semesters or with limited sections), I need to take a heavier-than-normal load this semester so that I can enroll in the correct sequence of courses and avoid delaying my graduation by an additional term.

Specifically, I am planning to take a combination of required MIS courses (including Systems Analysis and Design and Business Data Communications), a required Business core course (Operations Management), and two courses that satisfy both my Data Analytics minor and free electives. If I do not take all of these courses this semester, I will be forced to push at least one of the MIS courses to a later term. Because those MIS courses are prerequisites for the capstone and are only offered in specific semesters, that would mean postponing my graduation by at least one semester, potentially two if there are enrollment caps.

Academically, I believe I am well prepared to handle this excess credit load. My current cumulative GPA is 3.42, and I have consistently taken 15–16 credits per semester while maintaining strong grades. I currently work about 10–12 hours per week at the IT Support Center on campus and have already arranged with my supervisor to keep my work schedule flexible during exam weeks and major project deadlines. I also commute from an off-campus apartment within a 10-minute walk of Eberly, which minimizes time lost to transportation.

To prepare for this heavier course load, I have already started mapping out a detailed weekly schedule that blocks out specific times for reading, programming work, group projects, and exam prep. I have found that using tools like Trello and Google Calendar to track deadlines has helped me manage multiple demanding courses in previous semesters—for example, last spring I successfully completed 16 credits including a programming-intensive course (IFMG 250) and a writing-intensive Business Communications course while maintaining an A- average.

In addition, several of the courses I am requesting to take together are thematically related, which I believe will actually help me integrate the material rather than feel overwhelmed. For instance, the content in Systems Analysis and Design will directly support the project work in Business Data Communications, and both tie into the analytical skills I will be practicing in the Data Analytics and Advanced Excel course. I have also spoken informally with students who have taken these courses, and I understand the workload expectations and major assignments, so I am going into this with a realistic understanding of what will be required.

Overall, the excess credit request is motivated by a clear academic plan and an immediate degree completion timeline rather than a desire to overload without purpose. Approving this request will allow me to:

1. Complete all required prerequisites for my MIS capstone on time;
2. Finish the remaining courses for my Data Analytics minor without extending my time to graduation;
3. Stay on track for my intended graduation date at the end of the next academic year.

I understand that taking an excess credit load is a serious commitment, and I am prepared to prioritize my academic responsibilities, adjust my work hours if necessary, and use campus resources such as tutoring, faculty office hours, and the Writing Center to ensure that I maintain my academic performance while carrying this heavier schedule.
